title: Out-of-Bounds Access (Mission Governance at the PS)
description: Missions are immutable (§1279) and the resource never evaluates mission content — only the PS has the mission description, tool list, and log (§1193). Mission-level enforcement happens at the PS's token endpoint (§781, §1283, §1748). When an agent requests a scope that is inside its resource's policy but outside the approved mission, the resource still issues a resource token; the PS is the party that detects the mismatch, and per §797 responds with `202 + requirement=interaction` asking the user for consent. If the user declines, the agent must propose a new mission with a new s256.

mission_blob.title: New Mission (M2) issued after user declined the out-of-scope consent
mission_blob.description: Because the agent's broader request fell outside the original mission's scope and the user preferred a new mission over extending the old one, the agent proposes a fresh mission with a distinct s256.
mission_blob.markdown:
# Analyze Q2 Customer Feedback (Analytics Extension)

Read customer feedback records and detailed analytics data to produce a comprehensive report.

steps.label: POST /authorize (scope=analytics:read) + M1 → 200 resource token

tokens.name: Resource Token (carries M1 mission + broader scope)

annotations: Agent asks the resource for a resource token with scope="analytics:read", still referencing its original mission M1 (s256=R9kNpXeErMQ1Jvk_…).
The RESOURCE does not have the mission description or tool list — only the PS has those (§1193).
The resource evaluates against ITS OWN scope policy, decides analytics:read is a valid scope for analytics endpoints, and issues the resource token (§652).
The mission object {approver, s256} is stamped into the resource token as OPAQUE metadata — the resource does not judge whether the scope aligns with the mission's intent. That is the PS's job.

steps.label: POST /token → PS evaluates mission → 202 requirement=interaction

request_body.justification: Need detailed analytics to complement the feedback summary.

annotations: This is where mission governance actually happens (§781, §1283, §1748).
The PS has the full mission blob (description + approved_tools) AND the mission log. It compares the request against what the user approved.
The scope analytics:read and the path /data/detailed-analytics are not covered by mission M1's approved tools (FeedbackReader, ReportWriter).
Per §797, requests NOT within existing mission scope require user consent — so the PS returns 202 with requirement=interaction (§843, §849).
The PS has NOT federated to the AS. No auth token is issued while the mission-scope consent is pending.

steps.label: User reviews out-of-scope request → declines
steps.response_body:
Agent requests scope outside the current mission

The agent aauth:local@agent.example is operating under the mission Analyze Q2 Customer Feedback (approved with tools: FeedbackReader, ReportWriter).

It is now asking for:

  • scope: analytics:read
  • resource: /data/detailed-analytics
  • justification: “Need detailed analytics to complement the feedback summary.”

This is outside the scope the user originally approved. You can:
annotations: User sees a consent prompt that makes the mission-scope mismatch explicit.
User has three valid responses per the spec: approve (adds to mission log — missions accumulate context per §1281), ask for clarification (§1281, §881), or deny.
For this demo the user chooses Deny — they prefer a clean new mission over stretching the existing one, consistent with the immutability framing in §1279.

steps.label: Poll /pending/o4b1g2a7 → 403 access_denied

response_body.error_description: User declined the out-of-mission-scope consent. Request a new mission that covers the broader scope.

annotations: Agent learns that the PS's mission-scope consent check failed — the user declined.
The spec does not define a dedicated 'out-of-mission-scope' error code; the PS uses the standard access_denied pattern familiar from OAuth.
The original mission M1 is still ACTIVE (not terminated) — the agent can still operate within M1's original scope. It simply cannot broaden scope under M1.
To proceed with the analytics work, the agent must propose a NEW mission (§1279, missions are immutable).

steps.label: POST /mission (new M2 proposal, broader scope) → 202 pending

request_body.description:
# Analyze Q2 Customer Feedback (Analytics Extension)

Read customer feedback records and detailed analytics data to produce a comprehensive report.

annotations: Agent proposes a fresh mission covering the broader scope (analytics).
This is a NEW mission — not a mutation of M1. The approved M2 will have its own s256.
AAuth-Capabilities is NOT sent on PS endpoints (§1731).
PS returns 202 because it needs the user to review and approve the new mission (§1224).

steps.label: User reviews new mission → approves M2
steps.response_body:
Review New Mission

Analyze Q2 Customer Feedback (Analytics Extension)

The agent aauth:local@agent.example is requesting approval for a new mission covering the broader scope. The original mission M1 (feedback only) is unchanged and still active for its own scope.

Tools:

  • FeedbackReader — Read customer feedback records
  • AnalyticsReader — Read detailed analytics data
annotations: User reviews and approves the superseding mission M2.
PS records the approval, computes s256 over the new blob bytes.
M1 and M2 now coexist — the agent can operate under either, depending on the task.

steps.label: Poll /pending/m9b3c2a1 → 200 + new mission blob (M2)

response_body.description:
# Analyze Q2 Customer Feedback (Analytics Extension)

Read customer feedback records and detailed analytics data to produce a comprehensive report.

annotations: New mission M2 is approved. s256 = X7fE9m… — a fresh, distinct hash.
approved_at is new; the timestamp ensures s256 uniqueness (§1265).
Agent stores the new blob bytes exactly as received and verifies SHA-256(body) == s256 (§1273).

From here, the agent repeats the authorization flow (POST /authorize + M2 → resource_token → POST /token at PS → auth_token → GET /data/detailed-analytics) — now in scope of M2. See the Resource Access demo for the remaining hops.

events.label: Resource issues resource token
events.detail: Resource uses its OWN scope policy. Mission is stamped as opaque {approver, s256} metadata.

events.label: PS asks user consent
events.detail: PS evaluates against M1's mission scope and log; out-of-scope — requirement=interaction

events.label: User declines scope extension
events.detail: User prefers a new mission over stretching M1

events.label: access_denied returned to agent
events.detail: M1 remains active for its own scope; agent must propose a new mission for broader work

events.label: New mission M2 proposed
events.detail: Proposal enters the approval flow; user review pending

events.label: User approves M2
events.detail: Approved with a distinct s256, covering the analytics scope

events.label: Agent holds M2
events.detail: Agent can now re-run the authorization flow and obtain an auth token for analytics
